title-group.article-title: Cluster training versus traditional resistance training on lower-limb muscle strength, jumping, and explosive power performance: a systematic review and meta-analysis

sec.title: Objective
sec.p: Cluster Training (CT) has been a potential advantage over Traditional Resistance Training (TT) in improving lower-limb performance remains controversial. This meta-analysis aimed to systematically compare the effects of CT and TT on lower-limb muscle strength, jumping, and explosive power performance in healthy adults, providing evidence-based guidance for the scientific design of strength training programs.

sec.title: Methods
sec.p: A systematic search was conducted in PubMed, Embase, Cochrane Library, and Web of Science databases for randomised controlled trials published up to July 15, 2025. Eligible studies included healthy adults who underwent resistance training involving lower-limb exercises for at least four weeks, with the primary difference between groups being the set configuration. Meta-analysis, subgroup analysis, sensitivity analysis, and publication bias within a random-effects model framework using standardised mean differences assessment were performed using Review Manager 5.4 and Stata 16 software.

sec.title: Results
sec.p: A total of 25 studies comprising 573 participants were included. Pooled results indicated no statistically significant difference between CT and TT in lower-limb muscle strength (SMD = -0.06; 95% CI: -0.29 ~ 0.17; p = 0.59) or jumping performance (SMD = 0.20, 95% CI: − 0.07 ~ 0.47, p = 0.14). However, CT demonstrated a small effect size on lower-limb explosive power performance compared with TT (SMD = 0.28, 95% CI: 0.07 ~ 0.50, p = 0.01). Subgroup analyses revealed that in studies with longer training durations (9–12 weeks) and athletic populations, CT elicited smaller gains in lower-limb strength than TT. Sensitivity analyses confirmed the robustness of the findings, and publication bias tests showed no significant bias.

sec.title: Conclusion
sec.p: CT appears to be a viable alternative to TT with small advantages for explosive power performance under certain conditions, while strength and jump performance are broadly comparable. In long-term interventions and among athletes, CT may induce smaller improvements in strength compared with TT. Therefore, training goals, duration, and population characteristics should be carefully considered when incorporating CT into practice. Future research should further explore the long-term neuromuscular adaptations and individualized optimization of CT programs.

sec.title: Trial registration
sec.p: https//www.crd.york.ac.uk/PROSPERO/view/CRD420251090980.

sec.title: Supplementary Information
sec.p: The online version contains supplementary material available at 10.1186/s13102-026-01550-x.

sec.title: Introduction
sec.p: Resistance training (RT) has been widely recognised as an effective strategy to enhance muscular strength, athletic performance, and overall physical health [1–3]. Training of set configuration has been a key variable in moderating its physiological adaptations and functional gains. Cluster Training (CT) is a resistance training method that divides repetitions within a set into smaller “clusters,” with short planned rest intervals (10–45 s) between clusters. Traditional resistance training (TT) employs a continuous training format, where repetitions are performed sequentially without planned rest intervals between sets. CT focuses on reducing acute fatigue while maintaining exercise speed and power output during training [4, 5]. These include repeated inter-repetition rest (IR), repeated inter-repetition intervals (IRR), rest redistribution (RR), and equal work-to-rest (EW: R) configurations [6, 7]. All formats retain the core design principle of “short intra-set recovery intervals” [8].

Lower-limb resistance training highly depends on rapid force output, recruitment of high-threshold motor units, and the phosphagen energy system. Fatigue accumulated during continuous repetition could impair neuromuscular function through mechanisms such as accumulation of metabolic byproducts, reduced velocity of conduction in muscle fibers, and weakened discharge frequency of motor units, ultimately leading to a decline in velocity and power output [9]. Since velocity and power are key determinants of long-term adaptations in strength and explosive power performance, training strategies that decelerate the rate of velocity decline hold significant practical value. Some studies indicate that CT is more effective than TT in maintaining velocity and power output [10, 11], while others suggest minimal differences between the results [7] or that these differences are influenced by contextual factors such as load, rest structure, and training level [12]. Therefore, the mechanisms by which short rest periods induce fatigue resistance require further clarification.

Current studies have explored the practical applications of CT for strength [13], speed [10], muscle hypertrophy [14], and athletic performance [11]. However, few studies analysed lower-limb strength training effects individually or considered conditioning factors. The lower-limb musculature is the core driver of whole-body power output. Its strength and explosive power performance not only determine athletic performance in events like sprinting and jumping but also directly impact the safety of daily functional activities. Therefore, this systematic review and meta-analysis focus on lower-limb outcome measures and include moderator factor analysis to identify whether factors such as training duration, training level, or set configuration influence the relative effects of CT and TT, providing evidence-based guidance for optimising strength training protocol design.

sec.title: Methods
sec.p: This meta-analysis was conducted in accordance with the Preferred Reporting Items for Systematic Reviews and Meta-Analyses (PRISMA) guidelines [15] and the methodological standards outlined in the Cochrane Handbook for Systematic Reviews of Interventions [16]. The review protocol was prospectively registered in the PROSPERO database (registration number: CRD420251090980).

sec.title: Search strategy
sec.p: A comprehensive search was performed in PubMed, Embase, Cochrane Library, and Web of Science databases. Clinical trial registries and grey literature were not included in the search. Searches were conducted from the inception of each database up to July 15, 2025. In addition, reference lists of included studies and relevant systematic reviews and meta-analyses were manually screened to identify potential missed studies. The strategy search for each database is provided in the supplementary materials.

sec.title: Eligibility criteria
sec.p:
Study selection followed the PICOS framework, with only studies were included if they met all of the following criteria:
Population: Healthy adults aged ≥ 18 years without lower-limb functional impairments or clinical diagnoses affecting neuromuscular or joint function; Participants include non-resistance-trained individuals, regularly resistance-trained individuals, and athletes (trained in competitive sports for ≥ 1 year and having participated in formal competitions at the district level or higher); No gender or sport restrictions apply; Individuals with any clinical disease are excluded.
Intervention: Intervention group is CT, which involves brief intra-set rest intervals during resistance training; Training duration lasting at least four weeks, according to previous studies [7]; Training protocol should include lower-limb resistance training exercises, including plyometric movements utilising the stretch–shortening cycle.
Comparison: Control group is TT, which continuous resistance training is performed without intra-set rest intervals; Set configuration was the main differentiating variable; Training volumes were matched, and studies that did not match were labelled as “unmatched studies”. Their effect on the pooled effect was assessed through sensitivity analysis.
Outcomes: ①Lower-limb muscle strength refers to the maximum contraction capacity of muscles to overcome resistance, with outcome measures included maximal repetition strength and maximal eccentric strength;②Lower-limb jumping performance refers to the ability to synergistically generate elastic potential energy and rapid forces from lower-limb muscle groups, with outcome measures included Squat Jump, Counter Movement Jump (CMJ), and Standing Long Jump (SLJ)༛③ Lower-limb explosive power performance refers to the ability of the lower-limb to produce work per unit time, with outcome measures included: power output from weight-bearing exercises primarily involving lower-limb muscle strength, peak power during squat jumps, and sprint performance. When reporting multiple outcomes for the one study, our following decision rules apply: Firstly, prioritize the most widely accepted and validated outcomes from research (e.g., using 1RM squat for strength assessment; CMJ for jump performance; peak power for explosive power performance assessment); Secondly, when multiple equally valid outcomes exist, select the one with the highest methodological quality and most comprehensive report.
Study design: Randomized controlled trials (RCTs).
Studies were excluded if they met any of the following conditions:

list-item.p: Conference abstracts, dissertations, brief reports, or studies without full-text availability;
Outcome data could not be extracted or obtained from the authors;
The intervention did not include lower-limb resistance exercises;

sec.title: Risk of bias
sec.p: Two independent reviewers (ZK, MMZ) assessed the risk of bias for each included study using the Cochrane Collaboration’s Risk of Bias Tool (RoB 1) for randomised controlled trials, as described in the Cochrane Handbook [16]. The following domains were evaluated: random sequence generation, allocation concealment, blinding of participants and personnel, blinding of outcome assessment, incomplete outcome data, selective reporting, and other potential sources of bias [17]. Based on predefined criteria [18], each element was thoroughly assessed and classified as high risk, low risk, or unclear. This rigorous risk assessment process ensures the study’s credibility and upholds the scientific integrity of the findings.

sec.title: Data extraction
sec.p: Two independent reviewers (ZK, MMZ) performed the study selection and data extraction. The extracted information included: the first author’s name, year of publication, participant characteristics, details of the training programs, and outcomes related to lower-limb muscle strength, jumping, and explosive power performance. For each included study, the mean and standard deviation (SD) of pre- and post-intervention measurements were collected for all relevant outcomes. When multiple outcome measures were reported within a single study, results were prioritized according to their relevance to lower-limb strength and performance testing and their methodological similarity to conventional training assessments. Any discrepancies between the two reviewers were resolved through discussion, and if disagreement persisted, a third reviewer (X-J) was consulted for adjudication. For studies where complete outcome data were not directly available from the published text or supplementary files, attempts were made to contact the corresponding authors to obtain the missing information. Studies were excluded if the necessary data could not be obtained after these efforts.

sec.title: Statistical analyses
sec.p: where is the SD of the difference in means, is the SD from pre-intervention, is the SD from post-intervention, and is the correlation coefficient [17]. Correlation coefficients for pre- and post-intervention were rarely reported in the included studies and were generally assumed to be r = 0.50, as suggested by the Cochrane Handbook [17].

Because the outcome variables were continuous and measured using different scales or units across studies, standardised mean differences (SMDs) with 95% confidence intervals (95% CIs) were used to express the pooled effects. A p-value ≤ 0.05 was considered statistically significant. The smallest worthwhile change (SWC) defined as 0.2 times the standard deviation. When the standardised effect size (SMD) is ≥ the corresponding threseline is considered positive effect. Between-study heterogeneity was assessed using the I² statistic, where I² < 50% was interpreted as low heterogeneity, 50% ≤ I² < 75% as moderate heterogeneity, and I² ≥ 75% as substantial heterogeneity. Given the expected variations in study design, intervention protocols, and participant characteristics, a random-effects model was applied to calculate all pooled effect sizes. Forest plots were generated to visualize the magnitude and direction of pooled SMDs, with thresholds of < 0.5, 0.5–0.8, and ≥ 0.8 representing small, moderate, and large effects, respectively.

Sensitivity analyses were conducted to examine the robustness of the results by sequentially omitting individual studies and comparing the overall effects. In cases of substantial heterogeneity (I² ≥ 50%), potential sources of heterogeneity were further explored using prespecified subgroup analyses based on study characteristics. Publication bias was evaluated both qualitatively and quantitatively using Begg’s test, Egger’s regression test, and visual inspection of funnel plots.

To further investigate moderators influencing the effects of CT, subgroup analyses were performed to examine the potential moderating impact of key variables, including age, gender, training levels, duration, outcome indicators, and cluster types. Each subgroup was categorised based on the mean value of each variable to ensure meaningful between-group comparisons. Participants were classified as athletes (those engaged in athletic competition) or non-athletes (including university students, trained enthusiasts, or healthy adults without competitive experience).

CT configurations were further categorized into five structural subtypes according to previous literature [19]: Intra-repetition rest (IR): Short rest periods (10–45 s) inserted within each set; Intra-repetition rest with repetition intervals (IRR): Rest intervals added between individual repetitions within a set, while maintaining the same inter-set rest period; Rest redistribution (RR): Inter-set rest periods redistributed into shorter but more frequent rest intervals, keeping the total rest duration constant; Rest-pause (RP): Brief rest periods applied after reaching momentary failure to allow partial recovery and completion of additional repetitions; Equal work-to-rest ratio (EW: R): Work and rest durations are matched across protocols, allowing different total repetitions but maintaining a consistent work-to-rest ratio.

sec.title: Study selection
sec.p: A total of 2,376 records were identified through database searches, and 12 additional studies were retrieved through reference screening, resulting in 2,388 records in total. After removing 1,105 duplicates, 1,222 studies were excluded based on titles and abstracts, and 36 were excluded after full-text screening. Finally, 25 randomized controlled trials met the inclusion criteria and were included in the meta-analysis. The study selection process is illustrated in Fig. 1.

caption.p: Flow chart of the studies selection process

sec.title: Basic characteristics of included studies
sec.p: This meta-analysis included 25 studies comprising 573 participants. Most participants were young adults aged between 20 and 30 years, with only two studies involving individuals over 60 years old. The majority were non-athletic but resistance-trained adults, and both males and females were represented. Training durations ranged mainly from 4 to 8 weeks, with most interventions conducted twice per week. Among the cluster types, IR and RR were the most frequently applied, whereas RP were less common, likely due to their proximity to muscular failure. Most training interventions consisted of comprehensive resistance programs incorporating both upper and lower-limb exercises, while nine studies implemented single-exercise movements. The detailed characteristics of the included studies are summarized in Table 1.

sec.title: Results of ROB assessment
sec.p: The methodological quality of the included studies was evaluated using the Cochrane Handbook for Systematic Reviews of Interventions risk of bias tool [44]. The detailed assessment results are presented in Figs. 2 and 3. Eight studies explicitly described the method of random sequence generation, and three reported allocation concealment. None of the studies implemented blinding of participants, whereas fifteen studies described blinding of outcome assessors. Regarding data completeness, studies with an attrition rate greater than 20% without intention-to-treat analysis were considered at high risk of bias. Three studies had attrition rates exceeding 20%, while the remaining twenty-one reported comparable dropout rates and reasons between experimental and control groups, indicating generally good data integrity. Regarding reporting bias, three studies had ambiguously described outcomes. Regarding other bias, potential risks were identified in studies with baseline imbalances, possible conflicts of interest, or methodological issues related to sample size or study design. Based on the predefined quality criteria, fourteen studies were classified as high quality, seven as moderate quality, and four as low quality. Based on the above criteria, the included studies were judged to have a generally low risk of bias overall.

caption.p: Proportional representation of risk of bias assessment in included trials
Schematic representation of risk of bias assessment for included trials

sec.title: Effects of cluster training on lower-limb muscle strength
sec.p: A total of 23 studies involving 547 participants assessed lower-limb muscle strength. Among these, nineteen reported outcomes of maximal repetition strength, while five examined maximal eccentric strength. The results of the random-effects model indicated no statistically significant difference between CT and TT regarding their effects on lower-limb strength (SMD = −0.06; 95% CI: −0.29 ~ 0.17; p = 0.59). Figure 4 presents the forest plot of the pooled analysis for lower-limb muscle strength. The heterogeneity test revealed low variability among the studies (τ²=0.13, I² = 41.5%, p = 0.02), but Cochran’s Q test reached statistical significance was observed. Subgroup analyses were conducted to explore the potential sources of heterogeneity, and the pooled effect sizes for each subgroup are summarised in Table 2.

caption.p: Forest plot showing the effects of cluster training on lower-limb muscle strength. Note: SMD > 0 indicates CT is superior for lower-limb muscle strength, while SMD < 0 indicates TT performs better
Subgroup analysis table for lower-limb muscle strength

sec.p: Regarding the training level, TT improves lower-limb muscle strength more effectively than CT among athletes, with statistically significant differences observed (SMD = − 0.73, 95% CI: − 1.31 ~ − 0.15, p = 0.01). However, there was no statistically significant difference observed in improvement in lower-limb muscle strength between CT and TT among non-athletes (SMD = 0.03, 95% CI: − 0.17 ~ 0.24, p = 0.74). Subgroup analysis revealed significant differences between training levels (p = 0.02). This interaction test was conducted using a random-effects model, suggesting that training level moderates the relative effects of CT.

Regarding the training duration, CT and TT demonstrated no significant difference in their effects on lower-limb muscle strength over 4–8 weeks (SMD = 0.07, 95%CI: −0.14 ~ 0.28, p = 0.52); but 9–12 weeks revealed that TT was better than CT in improving lower-limb muscle strength, showing a trend toward a difference (SMD = − 0.46, 95% CI: −0.92 ~ − 0.00, p = 0.05), though moderate heterogeneity was present (τ²=0.22, df = 6; I² = 59%). Sensitivity analysis, excluding one study [43], substantially reduced heterogeneity without changing the pooled effect’s direction, confirming the results’ robustness. Statistically significant differences were observed in the interaction effects between subgroups across different training duration (p = 0.04), this interaction test was conducted using a random-effects model, suggesting that the duration of the intervention was a key moderating factor, and that TT was more effective than CT in promoting strength gains during longer training periods.
Furthermore, subgroup analyses based on age, gender, outcome indicators, and cluster types demonstrated no significant differences between CT and TT in lower-limb strength.

sec.title: Effects of cluster training on jumping performance
sec.p: A total of nine studies involving 215 participants assessed jumping performance. Among these, eight studies reported outcomes of countermovement jump (CMJ), three examined standing long jump (SLJ), and one evaluated vertical jump (VJ) performance. The random-effects model revealed no statistically significant difference was observed between CT and TT in enhancing jumping performance (SMD = 0.20, 95% CI: − 0.07 ~ 0.47, p = 0.14). The heterogeneity test revealed very low between-study variability (τ²=0, I²=0%, p = 0.97), suggesting high consistency among the included studies. Figure 5 presents the forest plot of the pooled analysis for jumping performance.

Subgroup analyses showed no significant differences between CT and TT across age, sex, training status, intervention duration, or outcome measures, indicating that these factors did not moderate the effects of CT on jump performance. Pooled effect sizes for each subgroup are summarised in Table 3.

caption.p: Forest plot showing the effects of cluster training on jumping performance. Note SMD > 0 indicates CT is superior for jump performance, while SMD < 0 indicates TT performs better
Subgroup analysis table for jumping performance

sec.title: Effects of cluster training on explosive power performance
sec.p: A total of fifteen studies involving 340 participants assessed lower-limb explosive power performance. Among these, eight studies assessed power output from weight-bearing exercises primarily involving lower-limb muscle strength, four examined power during loaded or unloaded jumps, and three measured sprint performance. Results from the random-effects model revealed a significant difference between CT and TT (SMD = 0.28, 95% CI: 0.07 ~ 0.50, p = 0.01), with negligible heterogeneity (τ²=0, I²=0%, p = 0.67), indicating that CT produced significantly greater improvements in lower-limb explosive power performance compared with TT. The forest plot of the pooled analysis for l explosive power performance is shown in Fig. 6.

caption.p: Forest plot showing the effects of cluster training on power performance. Note: SMD > 0 indicates CT is superior for explosive power performance, while SMD < 0 indicates TT performs better

sec.p: Regarding participant gender, CT led to greater improvements in explosive power performance among males (SMD = 0.42, 95% CI: 0.07 ~ 0.77, p = 0.02), whereas no significant differences were observed in females (SMD = 0.18, 95% CI: − 0.28 ~ 0.64, p = 0.64) or mixed-gender samples (SMD = 0.14, 95% CI: − 0.23 ~ 0.51, p = 0.46). The subgroup analysis revealed that the difference in effect between the groups was not significant (p = 0.52), indicating that gender did not modify the impact of CT on explosive performance.

Regarding training status, CT significantly enhanced explosive performance in non-athletes (SMD = 0.25, 95% CI: 0.01 ~ 0.50, p = 0.04), whereas no significant difference was observed in athletic populations (SMD = 0.33, 95% CI: − 0.13 ~ 0.78, p = 0.20). The subgroup difference test revealed no statistical significance (p = 0.79).

Regarding participant age, individuals younger than 25 years exhibited significantly greater improvements in explosive power performance under CT compared with TT (SMD = 0.29, 95% CI: 0.02 ~ 0.56, p = 0.05), while no significant difference was found among participants older than 25 years (SMD = 0.24, 95% CI: − 0.12 ~ 0.60, p = 0.20). The subgroup difference test revealed no statistical significance (p = 0.81).

Regarding performance indicators, CT produced significantly greater improvements in loaded power-based exercises (SMD = 0.29, 95% CI: 0.00 ~ 0.57, p = 0.05), whereas no significant differences were observed in jump power under loaded or unloaded conditions (SMD = 0.33, 95% CI: − 0.13 ~ 0.78, p = 0.16) or in sprint performance (SMD = 0.15, 95% CI: − 0.37 ~ 0.66, p = 0.57). The subgroup differences were not found to be statistically significant (p = 0.87). In addition, subgroup analyses indicated that intervention duration and CT configuration did not significantly affect the comparative effects of CT and TT on explosive performance. Pooled effect sizes for each subgroup are summarised in Table 4.

caption.p: Subgroup analysis table for power performance

sec.title: Sensitivity analysis
sec.p: To verify the robustness of the meta-analysis results, a leave-one-out sensitivity analysis was performed to assess the influence of each individual study on the overall pooled effect size and heterogeneity. Meanwhile, to address heterogeneity arising from unequal training volumes between CT and TT, studies were systematically screened for load matching and training volume matching. Studies in which CT and TT differed in the total number of repetitions, number of sets, or prescribed training loads were classified as “unmatched studies” [21, 25, 28, 32, 36, 40, 45]. Leave-one-out sensitivity analysis showed that after sequentially excluding each individual study, the direction and significance status of the pooled effects for each outcome did not change substantially (Figs. 7, 8 and 9), these findings indicate that the results of the present meta-analysis are stable and reliable.

caption.p: Sensitivity analysis of the effects of cluster training on muscle strength
Sensitivity analysis of the effects of cluster training on jump performance
Sensitivity analysis of the effects of cluster training on explosive power performance

sec.title: Publication bias
sec.p: Publication bias was evaluated using funnel plots, Begg test, and Egger’s test to qualitatively and quantitatively assess the main outcome measures. Funnel plots were constructed for lower-limb muscle strength, jumping performance, and explosive power performance, comparing CT and TT. The distribution of the plots appeared to be symmetrical on the whole, which initially indicates a modest small-sample effect in lower-limb explosive power performance, suggesting a relatively low risk of publication bias (Figs. 10, 11 and 12).

caption.p: Funnel plot illustrating the effect of Cluster Training on Lower-limb Muscle Strength
Funnel plot illustrating the effect of Cluster Training on Jump performance
Funnel plot illustrating the effect of Cluster Training on explosive Power performance

sec.p: Further statistical analyses revealed no significant publication bias in lower-limb muscle strength (Begg test p = 0.673, Egger test p = 0.908) or jumping performance (Begg test p = 0.917, Egger test p = 0.170). However, lower-limb explosive power performance (Begg test p = 0.113, Egger test p = 0.047) suggesting potential publication bias. To further validate the robustness of these findings, trim-and-treat adjustment was applied to the indices that exhibited signs of bias. The trim-and-fill method interpolated missing data, with the resulting adjusted SMD = 0.275 (95% CI: 0.067–0.483). Results showed a mild small-sample effect, with corrected CT still showing a slight advantage in explosive performance. Results indicate that the effect sizes after trim-and- fill adjustment did not exhibit directional reversal compared to the primary findings, suggesting that the pooled results are robust and bias has limited impact on the overall conclusions. Therefore, the results are highly reliable and credible.

sec.title: Discussion
sec.p: This study aimed to compare the effects of CT and TT on lower-limb muscle strength, jumping performance, and explosive power performance in healthy adults. The pooled effect sizes revealed no statistically significant difference between CT and TT in terms of lower-limb muscle strength (SMD = −0.06; 95% CI: −0.29 ~ 0.17; p = 0.59) and jumping performance (SMD = 0.20, 95% CI: − 0.07 ~ 0.47, p = 0.14). However, CT demonstrated a small effect size advantage in lower-limb explosive power performance (SMD = 0.28, 95% CI: 0.07 ~ 0.50, p = 0.01). Sensitivity analyses did not alter the direction or significance of the effect. But given the non-trivial risk of bias and the Egger’s test signal for explosive power performance indicators suggesting small-sample effects, the certainty of evidence in this study is moderate.

sec.title: Lower-limb muscle strength
sec.p: This study demonstrated that no statistically significant difference was observed between CT and TT in enhancing lower-limb muscle strength, which is consistent with the results of previous research [7]. Subgroup analyses further revealed that TT was superior to CT in studies involving athletes (SMD = − 0.73) and in longer training interventions (SMD = − 0.46), findings that align with those reported by Cui [14]. However, this subgroup result is based on a small number of studies (k = 4), which should raise caution regarding potential false positives due to small-sample bias. Further large-scale studies are needed to validate this conclusion.

From the perspective of intervention duration, early increases in muscle strength primarily depend on neural adaptations, such as enhanced cortical drive, improved motor unit recruitment, and synchronization, which enhance contraction efficiency and coordination [46]. During the early training phase, CT may promote phosphocreatine(PCr) resynthesis through brief intergroup rest periods, reducing the accumulation of metabolic byproducts such as lactate and hydrogen ions, thus helping to sustain high-quality repetition output. This mechanism may allow CT to achieve comparable short-term strength improvements to TT [13, 14]. However, as training continues, strength development becomes increasingly dependent on muscle hypertrophy. Studies have shown that lower-limb muscles respond more effectively to high-load, low-repetition (1–6 reps per set) TT protocols [47], and that the relative strength gain in the lower-limbs is typically higher than that of the upper-limbs (by approximately 2%) [48]. CT may reduce the accumulation of metabolic stress and mechanical tension due to the inclusion of rest periods within sets, thereby resulting in weaker stimulation of muscle hypertrophy signals (such as the mTOR pathway and muscle protein synthesis) [49, 50]. This could explain why CT yields slower strength gains than TT during long-term training phases.

From the perspective of training level, athletes who have undergone long-term systematic resistance training already exhibit optimized neuromuscular efficiency, greater neural drive, and higher motor unit recruitment rates. Therefore, the “fatigue management” benefit of CT provides minimal additional stimulation for further adaptation. Moreover, intra-set rest in CT may attenuate metabolic and hormonal responses (e.g., testosterone and growth hormone secretion), which are key contributors to muscle hypertrophy [27, 51, 52]. Hansen similarly argued for the use of CT as an alternative approach for developing lower-limb force, but stated that it is less effective for promoting muscle strength [28].

Conclusively, this study found no statistically significant difference between CT and TT in overall lower-limb strength effects. However, TT showed statistically significant differences between athlete groups and in long-term training settings. Nevertheless, the subgroup analysis results for athletes and long-term training duration were based on limited studies, and the interaction effect size between subgroups was low. Therefore, the long-term efficacy of CT requires further investigation. The differences in lower-limb muscle strength progression between CT and TT may stem from their distinct adaptation mechanisms. CT maintains high-output performance in the short term by controlling fatigue within the population and optimizing neural activation quality, making it suitable for early stages of neural adaptation or non-athlete populations. In contrast, TT may offer greater advantages in promoting muscle hypertrophy and strength growth by accumulating higher metabolic stress and mechanical tension [53]. That suggests for untrained or amateur training groups, CT may can achieve strength increases comparable to TT while reducing fatigue. However, if the target is muscle hypertrophy and the intervention period exceeds 8 weeks, TT may be more advantageous.

sec.title: Jump performance
sec.p: This study showed no significant difference in improving jumping performance between CT and TT. Although CT demonstrated a small positive effect on jump height (SMD = 0.20), this result did not reach statistical significance (p = 0.14), which is consistent with the findings reported by Jukic and Marshall [2, 12]. Previous studies have suggested that the intra-set rest structure of CT enables participants to sustain a higher level of effort across repetitions, thereby facilitating greater explosive power performance and enhancing neuromuscular activation while reducing neuromuscular fatigue [5]. Maintaining training intensity may further promote neural adaptations, such as improved recruitment and synchronisation of motor units (particularly type II muscle fibres [54]), which could positively impact countermovement jump (CMJ) performance. This mechanism has been supported by several studies [22]. However, despite the experimental designs of CT and TT being comparatively similar in the included trials, some studies did not match the training volume, even though the present analysis controlled for total load. Training volume mismatch is one possible contributor to the non-significant difference in jump performance; other confounding factors such as limited sample size and heterogeneity in jump test methods should also be considered.

Subgroup analysis revealed slight variations in the impact of CT on different jump performance indicators. CT showed a modest positive impact on CMJ performance (SMD = 0.13), while a slight negative trend was evident for SLJ (SMD = −0.14). These differences may be related to the distinct biomechanical and neuromuscular demands of the two types of jump. CMJ requires the lower-limb to perform a counter-movement, effectively utilizing the elastic potential energy of tendons and the stretch reflex. Relevant studies confirm that CMJ performance closely correlates with the rate of strength development and power output capacity [55]. SLJ, as a horizontal jumping movement, exhibits greater modulation of jumping performance by factors such as coordination and technique. Enhancing jumping performance may require more specialized technical practice rather than merely increasing power output. While CT may help to sustain high output during repetitions, it is unlikely to have a direct impact on motor coordination or technical movement patterns.

Evidence regarding the relationship between CT and jump performance remains inconclusive. Moghadam reported similar improvements in back squat and SLJ between CT and TT [35], whereas Asadi found CT to be more effective at enhancing jump and agility [22]. This inconsistency may stem from differences in training specificity. For instance, Asadi’s training program did not include horizontal movement tasks, which might explain the lack of improvement in horizontal jump performance due to insufficient task-specific practice.

Conclusively, CT appears to induce a small, albeit non-significant, improvement in jumping performance. Its effectiveness may depend on the biomechanical and neuromuscular similarity between training exercises and the tested jump task. When the movement patterns and mechanical demands of training closely match those of the jump, CT’s advantages in maintaining output quality may be more effective in enhancing jump performance.

sec.title: Explosive power performance
sec.p: This study showed that CT significantly outperformed TT in enhancing lower-limb explosive power performance (SMD = 0.28, p = 0.01). This finding aligns with Jukic’s conclusion that CT enables athletes to maintain higher movement velocity and explosive power performance under submaximal loads, thereby improving performance efficiency [12]. Subgroup analyses revealed that males (SMD = 0.42, p = 0.02), non-athletes (SMD = 0.25, p = 0.04), and younger participants (SMD = 0.29, p = 0.05) experienced greater improvements in explosive power performance with CT than with TT. Additionally, CT showed significantly greater improvements in explosive power performance during loaded movements (e.g., weighted squats) than during jump or sprint performance (SMD = 0.29, p = 0.05). Additionally, CT showed significantly greater improvements in explosive power performance during loaded movements (e.g. weighted squats) than during jump or sprint performance (SMD = 0.29, p = 0.05). However, no significant differences were detected between subgroups, suggesting that CT enhances explosive power performance consistently across populations, or that the current evidence base is insufficient to detect small subgroup effects.

From the perspective of age, younger participants (age < 25) have superior aerobic metabolism and phosphocreatine (PCr) resynthesis capacity compared to middle-aged or older adults [54]. Given that intracluster rest in CT allows more complete PCr replenishment and faster clearance of metabolic by-products, it can reduce lactate accumulation and mitigate fatigue-induced declines in movement velocity [19]. Thus, CT may enhance metabolic recovery, enabling younger participants to sustain higher explosive power performance during resistance training and resulting in more pronounced improvements in explosive power performance.

From the perspective of training level, non-athletes tend to experience rapid velocity loss during continuous repetitions in TT due to their lower neuromuscular efficiency, limited motor unit recruitment and lower fatigue tolerance. In contrast, athletes already possess optimised neural drive and fatigue resistance, which reduces the relative advantage of CT. Furthermore, the lower density of CT may attenuate the progressive overload stimulus necessary for further adaptation [56]. Hansen reported that, eight weeks after the intervention, there were similar improvements in peak velocity and power in both the CT and TT groups of elite rugby players, although TT produced slightly greater strength gains (18.3% vs. 14.6%) [28]. Lawton observed no significant differences in bench press power between CT and TT among adolescent athletes. Although CT may reduce perceived exertion and help maintain performance stability [5], there is insufficient evidence to support additional explosive power performance benefits for trained athletes [19].

From the perspective of gender, males generally possess greater muscle cross-sectional area (particularly of type II fibers) and higher testosterone concentrations, enabling the maintenance of movement velocity under high-force conditions. The short intra-set rest intervals in CT help to delay velocity decay and preserve movement quality under heavy loads. Females tend to exhibit lower rates of strength gain and neuromuscular activation during resistance training [48], potentially limiting the extent of explosive power performance improvements. A recent systematic review reported that the effect size for resistance training adaptations was markedly lower in young women (ES = 0.54) than in young men (ES = 0.98) [57]. Therefore, the limited strength adaptations in females may partially account for their non-significant explosive power performance improvements with CT.

From the perspective of outcome indicators, CT produced greater gains in explosive power performance in loaded exercises than in jump or sprint performance. Loaded power-based exercises are usually carried out at moderate to high intensities (approximately 60–75% 1RM) and depend heavily on the ability to develop force quickly [46]. Continuous repetitions in TT often induce lower-limb fatigue and velocity loss (≈ 20–25%), thereby limiting explosive power performance. The short rest intervals in CT alleviate localized muscle fatigue, maintain contraction velocity, and improve both mean and peak power output. In contrast, jump and sprint performance depend more on elastic energy utilization and rapid neuromuscular activation. Although CT can attenuate fatigue, its benefits may not directly translate to these skill-dependent movements, which require technical and coordination-specific training stimuli.

Explosive power is a product of force and velocity. CT to mitigate fatigue by optimizing set structure has been considered an effective strategy for delaying velocity and power decline [57], particularly evident under moderate to high load conditions. This provides theoretical support for CT enhancing explosive power performance [58]. Therefore, resistance training designed want to improve explosive power performance may be facilitated by using 70–80% of 1RM, with inter-set of 10–45 s rest at 2–3 repetitions, which promotes explosive power performance enhancement. In conclusion, this study observed a statistically significant advantage of the CT benefit was more prominent among males and non-athletes, while CT also demonstrated a slight advantage in explosive power performance during loaded movements. However, the small effect size and potential publication bias must be considered. Future research requires larger sample sizes and mechanistic measurements to validate the moderating effects of training level, gender, and task type on the effect.

Previous reviews have supported considerable superior effects of CT on strength, power, or fatigue-related metrics. This review provides a more focused and detailed analysis by concentrating on lower-limb performance —including maximum strength, jumping and explosive power performance—delivering precise evidence for resistance training program design and future research directions. Additionally, this study examined training level, age, gender, intervention duration, and cluster configuration through the analysis of moderating variables, in order to more precisely elucidate the potential marginal advantages of cluster training under specific conditions. Specifically, under conditions of matched load, CT can serve as an alternative to TT to improve lower-limb explosive power performance, with specific training contexts.

sec.title: Limitation
sec.p: This study systematically evaluated the effects of CT and TT on lower-limb muscle strength, jumping, and explosive power performance in healthy adults. However, due to limitations in the characteristics of the included studies and the methodological properties of the meta-analysis, the following constraints remain:

First, most included participants were young, non-athletic males, while data on trained athletes and older adults were scarce. Moreover, the training programs varied considerably across studies in terms of exercise selection, frequency, and duration. Although subgroup analyses provided more detailed insights, exact matching of training volume was not always feasible. Therefore, subtle differences in total training load might have contributed to variations in outcomes and should be examined in future studies.

Second, the methodological quality assessment revealed potential or unclear risks of bias, particularly in allocation concealment and performance blinding. These limitations are largely inherent to exercise intervention studies, where complete blinding is difficult to achieve, but they may nonetheless introduce bias and affect the objectivity of the results.

Third, while subgroup analyses partially explained the observed heterogeneity (e.g., training status, intervention duration, and performance indicators), residual heterogeneity remained. This may be attributed to variations in the CT themselves, including differences in set structure, training load, and intra-set rest intervals, which may have introduced uncertainty into the pooled estimates.

Future studies should adopt more rigorous methodological designs, including improved allocation concealment and blinding procedures, standardized training programs, and consistent outcome measures. Additionally, more high-quality, long-term randomized controlled trials targeting specific populations are needed to further verify and refine the present findings.

sec.title: Conclusion
sec.p: This systematic review and meta-analysis demonstrated that CT produced significantly greater improvements in lower-limb explosive power performance compared with TT, while no significant differences were observed between CT and TT in enhancing lower-limb muscle strength and jumping performance. Subgroup analyses further revealed that TT elicited superior strength gains in athletes and during longer intervention periods. These findings suggest that CT represents an efficient and strategically valuable form of resistance training. When appropriately tailored to individual characteristics and training goals, CT can effectively mitigate fatigue accumulation through the use of intra-set rest intervals, thereby maintaining higher movement velocity and power output throughout training sessions. Nevertheless, certain limitations(such as study quality and incomplete matching of training volume)should be acknowledged. Future research should incorporate neural and physiological measurements to clarify the specific mechanisms underlying CT adaptations across different training durations and participant populations, ultimately providing stronger evidence for the development of individualized and optimized strength training programs.
